data.title: War Scenarios: A Guide For Investors
data.description:
In the early morning hours of June 13, which was early evening in the US, Israel launched a series of preemptive strikes on Iran. This marks the start of a conflict that's likely to last several weeks, at minimum. The war could have profound implications for the global economy.

In this article, I will lay out scenarios that investors should contemplate as they plan how to manage their portfolios in response to this crisis.

A Review of Facts

Let us briefly review the facts as they stand.

1. Coordinated air, sea and land strikes. Israel has hit key Iranian targets with a series of precision aerial strikes, including piloted air strikes and unmanned strikes with missiles and drones. Attacks were also launched from the sea, including ships and submarines. In addition, attacks were carried out on the ground by Israeli commandos and intelligence operatives within Iran.

2. Decapitation. The strikes have been extremely successful in decapitating the entire top echelon of Iranian military leadership.

3. Key facilities struck. Initial attacks have focused on weakening Iranian defenses and retaliation capabilities by taking out air defense systems and missile launching systems.

4. Nuclear facilities hit. The Israelis have hit several sites that are key to Iran's nuclear program, but these hits have only been preliminary, as Iran's facilities are scattered all over the country and many are deep underground.

5. Iran has vowed revenge on both Israel and the US. Iranian officials have stated that they plan not only to carry out devastating attacks on Israel but that they also will hold the US responsible for enabling the strikes.

6. President Trump Supports Israel. Despite fierce criticism from many right-wing figures, President Trump called the strikes "excellent," and that there would be "more to come." And he has said that the US will not only protect itself if attacked but will protect Israel if called upon to do so. Trump has also said that Iran squandered an opportunity to negotiate a deal, but that the door is still open.

Scenarios for Investors

Despite the fact that investors will be confronted with a barrage of news connected with the war between Israel and Iran, investors only need to focus on a few things.

1. Closure Strait of Hormuz. By far, the most consequential potential event in this war, from the point of view of the global economy, would be if Iran decides to block passage of commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. As I have explained in detail in my article, "Oil Price Shock: The Path to $300," a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz would constitute a disruption in global oil supplies that's greater than that caused by the Arab Oil Embargo in 1973-1974. The relatively modest disruption at that time caused a quadrupling of global oil prices. A GAO study cited in that article estimates that a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz would cause a similar quadrupling of global oil prices. US generals have assessed that Iran does, indeed have the capacity to block the Strait of Hormuz. Furthermore, recent military actions by both Iran and the Houthis confirm that the possess the military capability to successfully stop virtually all traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. It's important to understand that Iran does not need to confront US naval vessels at sea. It will suffice that threaten to sink any ship that attempts to pass through the Strait. Under such conditions, no insurer will insure vessels passing through the Strait and no shipping company will allow its ships to pass through the Strait. For those interested in more details on the strategic military situation associated with the Strait of Hormuz, I cover this topic extensively in my SA blog post, "Iran Can Crash Global Markets: Blocking the Strait of Hormuz."

2. Attacks on oil facilities in the region. Iran has indicated that in the event of war they may choose to attack oil facilities in the region, including ports, production and processing facilities. Iran possesses the capability to inflict major damage on oil infrastructure in the Middle East through use of its drones, missiles and hypersonic missiles. In particular, air defenses at oil facilities in the Middle East do not have the capacity to withstand attacks from hypersonic missiles. Iran has previously shown a willingness to attack oil infrastructure in the region by having supported an armed Houthi attacks on the giant Abqaiq-Khurais facility in Saudi Arabia.

3. Attacks on US Military Facilities. Iran has previously threatened to attack US military facilities throughout the Middle East in the event of a conflict. Since Iran has held the US co-responsible for the strikes on Iran, it's likely that US military facilities, and possibly even civilian facilities, will be hit. If this is the case, this would draw the US more deeply into the war, with important economic implications, including a ballooning of US deficits as a result of increased military expenditures.

4. Activation of sleeper terrorist cells in the US. For many years it has been speculated that Iran possesses sleeper cells in the US that are tasked with carrying out terrorist attacks on US soil, upon command. While such reports are speculative, this possibility of terror attacks on US soil should not be discounted, as they are taken seriously by the FBI. Of particular concern to investors would be attacks on sensitive economically important targets and infrastructure.

What Should Investors Do?

In our view, the initial reaction of markets has grossly underestimated the potential economic and financial impact of the war. Initial spectacular Israeli successes have potentially lifted hopes that Iran's capacity to respond militarily have been so severely degraded that many of the risk listed above will not materialize.

In our view, risk is asymmetric. While everything could turn out fine and US equity markets could rally by 5%-10% on good news, the potential downside from this conflict for US equities is in the 30%-50% range.

In our view, investors would be wise to consider lowering risk in their portfolios. Investors should further consider capitalizing on this situation by increasing short-side bearish exposure to US equities and by investing in equities that could potentially benefit from the conflict.

Final Thoughts

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we have been preparing for an Iran-Israel war for many months. We were positioned to profit from this war prior to the outset of this war and we will be making aggressive moves to capitalize on this situation in the days ahead, in accordance with our hour-by-hour assessment of developments.

data.title: Dollar Hits 3-Year-Low: Here's What It Means For Precious Metals (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

The U.S. dollar is undergoing a major technical breakdown—a highly bullish development for commodities, especially precious metals and their mining stocks.

For the past couple of months, I’ve been warning of a likely breakdown in the U.S. dollar—an event that historically signals a bullish turn for precious metals due to their well-established inverse relationship (read my recent report to learn more).

That breakdown is now unfolding, as I had anticipated, and gold, silver, and mining stocks are responding favorably. In this update, I’ll break down where these assets currently stand from a technical perspective and what’s likely to come next.

In my recent dollar updates, I emphasized the importance of the 100 level on the U.S. Dollar Index—a key support that had triggered several major rallies in recent years. But that support was recently broken, shifting my bias firmly bearish. In my latest analysis, I also pointed out the 98 level as the next important support and warned that a break below it would confirm a deeper selloff. Sure enough, the index sliced through that level today, hitting a three-year low and further reinforcing the downtrend.

The U.S. dollar has weakened over the past two days, driven by several key developments. Yesterday’s benign Consumer Price Index (CPI) report was followed by a soft Producer Price Index (PPI) reading today, reinforcing the disinflationary trend.

Adding to the pressure, a new report yesterday showed that U.S. continuing jobless claims have risen to their highest level in over three years. Together, these data points increase the likelihood of upcoming interest rate cuts—further weighing on the dollar’s outlook.

The weekly chart of the U.S. Dollar Index highlights how the 100 level has served as critical support for several years, with multiple rebounds occurring off of it. However, the recent decisive break below that level has given an important bearish signal. With that breakdown now validated, the next major support lies near the 90 level—which is now my next downside target.

Zooming out to the long-term monthly chart of the U.S. Dollar Index reveals that it has been trading within a rising channel for nearly two decades. A continued move toward the key 90 level would mark a decisive breakdown from that pattern—one that is likely to open the door to even deeper declines.

There are numerous fundamental reasons to remain bearish, including the dollar's extreme overvaluation relative to historical norms. A sustained dollar bear market would be like rocket fuel for commodities, especially precious metals and mining stocks. As a reminder, the early 2000s commodities bull market was fueled, in large part, by a prolonged dollar bear market—and I see strong parallels between that period and the current environment.

Next, let’s take a look at gold via COMEX futures, which I follow closely due to its tendency to trade in clean $100 increments—creating strong support and resistance around round numbers like $3,000, $3,100, $3,200, and so on.

Gold delivered a strong performance today, rising 1.76% on the back of soft inflation and jobs data, which pressured the U.S. dollar. Geopolitical tensions also contributed to the rally, with reports indicating that Israel may be weighing a strike on Iran’s nuclear facilities. This surge pushed gold futures up to the $3,400 level—but for a stronger bullish confirmation, I’d like to see a decisive breakout above that threshold in the days ahead.

As I’ve been showing in recent updates, gold remains in a healthy consolidation range between $3,200 and $3,500, pausing to catch its breath after strong gains earlier this spring.

This sideways action may continue a bit longer, but a decisive breakout above $3,500 is the key signal I’m watching. That would likely mark the start of gold’s next major leg higher—likely targeting $4,000 next, given its significance as a major psychological milestone that tends to act like a magnet for price.

It’s also worth noting that gold had been forming a bull flag pattern over the past couple of months—and it recently broke out to the upside. This is a technically bullish development that reinforces the strength of the current move and warrants close attention.

I’ve recently begun tracking gold priced in the World Currency Unit (WCU)—a composite currency based on the GDP-weighted average of the world’s 20 largest economies. In many ways, it offers one of the most balanced and accurate reflections of gold’s true global performance, which is why I’ve been paying close attention to it.

Gold priced in World Currency Units (WCUs) clearly demonstrates that its uptrend remains strong, with a well-defined trading range between 2,400 and 2,600 that’s important to watch closely:

Silver has finally broken through its long-standing $34–$35 resistance zone—a strong signal that its bull market is gaining momentum after a year of frustrating false starts. Despite a few quiet days, the breakout remains intact. In my view, silver is simply consolidating before its next leg higher—first to $40, and eventually much higher.

Next, let’s take a look at the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI)—a proprietary indicator I developed to help validate silver’s price action and filter out potential false moves. The SSPI is calculated as the average of gold and copper prices, with copper scaled by a factor of 540 to prevent gold from dominating the index. Interestingly, even though silver isn’t part of the calculation, the SSPI closely tracks its movements. To learn more, check out the recent article I published.

The SSPI remains in a consolidation range between 2,800 and 3,000. A breakout above the 3,000 level would be a strong bullish confirmation for silver. Recent bullish action in both gold and copper pushed the SSPI right up to that 3,000 mark—an encouraging sign—but I’m waiting for a decisive close above it to confirm the breakout. I’ll be watching this development closely and will keep you all updated as it unfolds.

I also like to examine the underlying components of the SSPI to get a clearer sense of what’s happening beneath the surface. Since we’ve already covered gold, let’s now take a quick look at copper.

Copper is just under a major resistance zone between $5.00 and $5.20. A breakout above that zone would likely mark the start of a new copper bull market—one that would lift the SSPI and, by extension, silver as well. That’s because algorithmic trading systems arbitrage price relationships across metals, often causing their movements to align.

Next, let’s look at gold mining stocks, as tracked by the popular large-cap VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). Notably, GDX recently broke above the $42–$46 resistance zone I’ve been highlighting—a strong signal that gold miners are finally coming to life.

After years of being deeply undervalued, mining stocks are finally starting to attract attention and get bought up. I’m increasingly optimistic about the sector and will be writing more soon now that I have a much clearer read on their trajectory.

Silver miners, tracked by the SIL ETF, are also performing strongly and have entered the key $48–$52 resistance zone. A decisive breakout above this zone would provide further bullish confirmation—especially as silver’s momentum continues to build following its recent breakout.

To summarize, the U.S. dollar is undergoing a major technical breakdown, driven by signs of easing inflation and a weakening job market. Given the long-established inverse relationship between the dollar and commodities—especially precious metals—this breakdown is likely to serve as a powerful tailwind for gold, silver, and their mining stocks.

It’s an exciting and pivotal moment for the precious metals space, and I’m glad to have you on this journey with me.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CALF: This Struggling Cash Cows ETF Needed A Strategy Change - And It Got One
data.description:
Investment Thesis

According to this S&P Dow Jones Indices news release from March 11, 2025, adjustments were made to the selection process for the Pacer US Small Cap Cash Cows ETF (BATS:CALF) after consultation with market participants. While it will take analyzing several quarterly reconstitutions to gauge the impact, I'm encouraged by the initial changes to CALF from a fundamentals perspective, as it's jumped from #30/34 to #10/34 among small-cap value ETFs in my factor-driven ETF Rankings System. Growth remains a challenge, as I noted in my last review from June 2024, but there are reasons to be optimistic that CALF can rebound after a challenging few years. I hope you enjoy the read, and I look forward to your questions afterward.

CALF Overview

CALF's New Approach: What Changed?

A previous article written by Sungarden Investment Publishing on March 16 reasoned that investors have stopped caring about small caps. Well, The good news for CALF shareholders is that it includes plenty more mid-caps than before because there was a major change to CALF's selection universe. After previously selecting from the S&P Small Cap 600 Index, CALF's new selection universe is the S&P United States SmallCap Index.

This one change has enormous implications. To illustrate, consider how the new selection universe is larger in number (2520 vs. 602 securities) and size, evidenced by metrics like the largest stock by market cap ($92.2 vs. $8.2 billion) and the average market cap ($3.4 vs. $2.3 billion). Furthermore, larger stocks are likely to generate more free cash flow dollars, which is how the Index weights its components. As a result, these larger companies will receive higher weightings, and CALF will begin to function more like a small/mid-cap fund rather than a straight small-cap fund like before.

Previously, CALF selected 100 securities with the highest free cash flow yield (free cash flow as a percentage of enterprise value) and capped stock weights at 2% at each quarterly reconstitution. The new strategy allows for slight deviations from that 2% cap, but more importantly, CALF will hold 200 securities moving forward. The obvious impact is increased diversification, which was one of the reasons for the change. The impact should be positive from a quality perspective and could open up new doors from a growth perspective as well.

CALF Performance Summary vs. Peer Group

In this article, I will compare CALF with four peers:

  1. iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS)
  2. Avantis US Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV)
  3. VictoryShares Small Cap Free Cash Flow ETF (SFLO)
  4. VictoryShares US Small Mid Cap Value Momentum ETF (USVM)

IJS is a well-known small-cap value benchmark with a 25-year track record and a relatively low 0.18% expense ratio. I think Avantis has proven itself to be a competent fund manager with how well AVUV has performed since its launch six years ago. SFLO is an ETF I've seen requested in the comments section of other articles, so I hope seeing how it compares with CALF will prove helpful. Lastly, USVM has jumped to #2/34 in my ETF Rankings for small-cap value stocks, and since it also includes some mid-cap stocks, it's a possible alternative for investors who may have grown too frustrated with CALF's recent performance.

On that note, CALF has struggled in recent years. As shown below, it built up a 54% lead over IJS up until the end of 2023. However, that lead has dwindled to only 17% (78.44% vs. 61.48%) through June 11, 2025. Earlier in 2023, I warned readers that CALF was starting to "grapple with slow sales and earnings growth." That's one of the main benefits of fundamental analysis - you can see the changes happening in real-time and learn what an ETF consistently does well and what it only sometimes does well.

We can better appreciate CALF's fall from grace when adding the other three ETFs to the mix. As shown below, CALF has declined by an annualized 11.09% since January 2024 compared to a -1.22% annualized loss for IJS. AVUV and SFLO managed small gains of 2.04% and 3.54%, respectively, but it was USVM that stood out with a 10.22% annualized return through June 11, 2025.

This might explain why Pacer felt the need to change gears, but it also demonstrates how we should not paint all small-cap value ETFs with the same brush. Consider how USVM leads the entire small-cap value category over the last two years, with a 35.35% total return through June 2025, but the Invesco S&P SmallCap High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (XSHD) lost 1.17% over the same period. CALF's total return was 7.21%, but that's not representative of the category at all, given how the average gain was 21.46%.

CALF Analysis

Factor Ranking Trends

I'm prepared to wipe CALF's performance slate clean, and I encourage others to do the same, especially once you consider what's happened to the portfolio from a factor perspective. I've pulled the following factor rankings for CALF over the last twelve months from my database, which I update for nearly 1,000 U.S. Equity ETFs each month. Have a quick scan - as I mentioned earlier, it's fascinating to see the changes month-to-month, especially after the strategy change occurred in time for the April 2025 rankings.

Depending on the month, these rankings are out of 34 or 35, and here are my quick takeaways:

1. CALF's liquidity rank improved because the liquidity of its underlying assets improved. This is referred to as an ETF's primary market liquidity, and the higher score makes sense because CALF no longer holds as many small-cap stocks that tend to be less liquid.

2. CALF's diversification rank improved from #29-30 in 2024 to #20-21 in 2025, though the improvement happened before the strategy change. I measure diversification based on an ETF's concentration in its top sub-industries, which I think is a nice compromise between company- and sector-level analysis. CALF's doubling of its holdings to 200 helped, but its top 25 sub-industries still comprise 73% of the portfolio compared to 55% for IJS, so I'll stop short of calling CALF well-diversified for the moment.

3. CALF's growth score hardly changed and is still at the bottom of the small-cap value category at #30/34 as of June 1, 2025. Fortunately, the greater lean into mid-cap stocks that to be more profitable improved CALF's quality rank to #1 in the small-cap value category. CALF even ranks #1 among value ETFs with weighted average market caps below $10 billion (which includes 11 mid-cap funds), so no matter how you slice it, CALF is up there as one of the highest-quality small/mid-cap value funds on the market.

4. CALF's value rank slightly deteriorated to #8, but that's because of the increase in the number of holdings from 100 to 200. As you increase the number of required selections, the value opportunities from #101-200 aren't as attractive, but you do it to satisfy another objective, like diversification. That's the calculation Pacer has made, and I still think #8/34 is sufficient.

5. The most dramatic improvements occurred in momentum and sentiment, which are based on statistics like short- and long-term price returns, earnings revisions, and sales and earnings surprises. Importantly, I calculate these at the individual stock level and weight them accordingly for each ETF because otherwise, I couldn't account for portfolio turnover.

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between CALF, IJS, AVUV, SFLO, and USVM. At the bottom is a sector diversification score that compares an ETF's allocations compared to hypothetical equal-weight-by-sector and single-sector portfolios. The higher the score, the more diversified, and in CALF's case, its 6.47/10 score is 0.22 points below the peer group average. It's not a bad result, but there are clearly better-diversified options.

CALF excludes stocks in the Financial sector by design, but it also has minimal or no exposure to defensive sectors like Consumer Staples and Utilities, potentially putting it at a disadvantage in uncertain markets. SFLO is the same way, so that's just a consequence of the free cash flow approach.

CALF's top ten holdings are next, and they comprise 20.38% of the fund as of June 13, 2025. Most of these are near the 2% weight cap, and for the most part, these are just the best performers since the latest reconstitution.

CALF Fundamental Analysis: Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for CALF's top 25 holdings, which total 41.32% on a cash-adjusted basis. The first takeaway is the fund's $7.26 billion weighted average market cap, which is substantially more than the $2.59 billion at the time of my review last year. There wasn't even a stock with a market cap above $7 billion at that time, but now, there are 13, and they comprise one-quarter of the fund.

Here are three additional takeaways:

CALF: Strong Momentum / Sentiment, But It Might Not Last

The last three columns include statistics that contribute to CALF's improved momentum and sentiment rankings. The first one I want to highlight is CALF's 48.89% annual price position, which is calculated for each stock using the following formula:

[52W High Price - 52W Low Price] / [Current Price - 52W Low Price]

This statistic essentially tells us where CALF's components trade in relation to their 52-week high and low prices, and in this case, it's about right in the middle. That's far different from the 28.01% figure from three months ago, and although it's challenging for any value ETF to also have really strong momentum features, I'm hoping the wider selection universe (2500 vs. 600 stocks) will help CALF avoid the really deeply-discounted plays that have strong free cash flow yields solely because they're so disliked.

CALF's stronger momentum features are well-supported by an outstanding 12.11% earnings surprise last quarter, led by CF Industries (CF), which surprised to the upside by 31.56% and is now trading close to its 52-week high price. CF Industries also has an "A+" EPS Revision Score, and CALF's overall 6.07/10 EPS Revision Score ranks the best in the peer group.

However, these strong statistics probably won't last because CALF doesn't screen for momentum or earnings revisions. I expect some of these high-performing stocks will get removed at the next quarterly reconstitution, scheduled to take effect on June 23, 2025, but I'm hoping the changes won't be too drastic.

CALF: Still Sticking To Its Roots With Excellent Value/Quality Combo

CALF's free cash flow margins are huge at 15.02% and about 2.5x IJS's, which represents the fund's benchmark, as listed on its fact sheet. SFLO is next at 12.44%, followed by USVM and AVUV at 10.23% and 7.48%, respectively. CALF also trades at only 11.41x forward earnings and 5.62x trailing cash flow, which is pretty close to the peer group average. Overall, the combination of high quality and low value is excellent, and that's what makes the free cash flow yield statistic so powerful.

CALF: Growth Remains Low

CALF's one-year estimated sales and earnings per share growth rates are low at 2.05% and 4.45%, respectively. Meanwhile, USMV's growth rates are much better at 6.84% and 10.47% and contribute to its higher overall ranking on my end. In my view, USVM's higher price multiples are worth it if it prevents severe underperformance, and I think, in general, most people don't want to buy stocks that aren't growing.

The lone bright spot for CALF regarding growth is that its weighted average three-year free cash flow growth rate is excellent at 15.41% compared to 4.68%, 6.46%, and 4.55% for IJS, AVUV, and USVM. SFLO comes in first place with a growth rate of 18.26%, so if you're open to it and like the free cash flow approach, I think owning both makes sense. The two only overlap by 34.11%, and with the two selecting from different universes, they should complement each other quite well moving forward.

Investment Recommendation

CALF's strategy change in March had huge implications for its shareholders. It now holds 200 stocks instead of 100, and its selection universe widened from 600 to 2,500, allowing it to own higher-quality and more liquid stocks many would classify in the mid-cap category while retaining its strong quality and value features. CALF also has solid momentum and sentiment features right now, and while I don't expect this to be consistent at each reconstitution, I'm hopeful the wider universe means CALF will steer clear of deeply discounted stocks that were only selected due to their terrible price returns.

In summary, my message to CALF shareholders is to hang tight but not get too comfortable. While I have reason to believe its poor recent returns won't continue, I need to see how the fund changes at each reconstitution from a fundamentals perspective, and unfortunately, that means more time. Therefore, I've decided to assign a "hold" rating for now, and I look forward to providing regular updates as the year progresses. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Sealed Air Corporation: It's Not A Wrap Yet
data.description:
Over two years ago, in January 2023, I decided to revisit Sealed Air Corporation (NYSE:SEE), a global packaging company that I had previously been bullish on. I decided, based on how shares were priced and in light of overall financial performance for the business leading up to that point, to reaffirm it as a ‘buy’ candidate. But since then, things have not gone exactly according to plan. While the S&P 500 has risen by 50.8% since then, shares of Sealed Air Corporation have seen a downside of 37.3%. This comes on the back of declining revenue, profits, and cash flows. The general weakness that the company has experienced has not been uniform, though. In fact, the most recent data, covering the first quarter of 2025, has shown some mixed results and, dare I say, improvements.

As an analyst, I am clearly disappointed with this outcome so far. Having said that, as a long-term investor, I believe that a focus on the long haul will ultimately yield positive results for most investors. It is true that the overall financial performance of the business has been underwhelming recently. But shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. Given these facts, I firmly stand by my previous ‘buy’ rating for the company. And as time goes on, I suspect that shares will achieve meaningful upside.

It's not a wrap

In case you are new to Sealed Air Corporation, the company operates in the global packaging solutions industry. It provides customers with a wide array of packaging solutions, including its world-famous bubble wrap. Historically, the company has focused on two primary markets. One of these is the food industry through its Food segment. This particular part of the company provides solutions that are sold to food processors in fresh red meat, smoked and processed meats, poultry, seafood, fluids and liquids, cheese, and other food markets across the globe. There is also the Protective segment, which provides packaging solutions for things like consumer goods, medical devices, pharmaceutical products, and even the industrial manufacturing industry.

Unfortunately, financial performance has not been that great recently. From 2023 to 2024, for instance, revenue contracted from $5.89 billion to $5.39 billion. During this time, the company suffered from a $118.8 million hit involving the pricing of its goods. Foreign currency fluctuations hit revenue by $35.7 million as well. Things would have been worse had it not been for the fact that it benefited to the tune of $34.7 million from higher volumes and to the tune of $23.5 million from acquisitions. This decline in revenue followed an even larger decline from $5.64 billion from 2022 to 2023. Though in that case, all the pain came from reduced volumes.

It does appear as though market conditions are improving. For instance, from 2022 to 2023, the lower volumes that the company experienced stemmed from market pressures and inventory destocking in the industrial and fulfillment industries. Food retail market declines also occurred because of supply chain disruptions. But from 2023 to 2024, the company saw, at least in the food category, attractive strength, with volumes growing by $137 million because of end market demand and competitive share gains. For the Protective segment, there has been some weakness still, really because of the same weakness in industrial and fulfillment end markets. Honestly, none of this is surprising to me. Years of inflationary pressures and high interest rates aimed at combating them have certainly dulled buying activity. And given the whole trade war situation that is going on now, I wouldn't be surprised to see that trend continue.

With revenue falling, profitability for the company also declined. Net income dropped from $341.6 million in 2023 to $264.7 million last year. On an adjusted basis, net profits inched lower from $461.3 million to $458.9 million. Other profitability metrics for the company ended up mixed. For instance, operating cash flow actually jumped from $516.2 million to $728 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we would get a decline from $717.9 million to $614.5 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company remained virtually unchanged at $1.11 billion.

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, we have seen continued weakness on the sales front. A $26.7 million decline in volume, which was driven by a $29 million hit from the Protective segment because of continued weakness in the fulfillment and industrial side of things, and a $1.1 million decline in product pricing, helped to push sales down from $1.33 billion to $1.27 billion. Part of the decline, actually the largest part, came from foreign currency fluctuations that negatively affected revenue by $29.9 million.

Even though revenue has been on the decline, profitability for the company improved drastically. Net income shot up from $82 million last year to $113.5 million this year. The company benefited during this time from a reduction in interest expense from $65.1 million to $56.8 million. But that wasn't all. You see, management has been working hard on restructuring initiatives. Back in August 2023, the company announced a three-year cost takeout program called the CTO2Grow Program. This has been a costly initiative aimed at improving operations from a profitability perspective. During the lifetime of the program, it was estimated at the time to cost shareholders $193 million. $160 million of that was in the form of cash costs. Year-over-year in the first quarter, restructuring expenses for the company contracted from $15.5 million to $2.6 million. But by that time, the company still acknowledged that it had about $30 million in additional costs to incur in order to see the program to fruition. If everything does go according to plan, the company should see annualized savings of between $140 million and $160 million.

The good news is that we are starting to see some benefit here. As an example, adjusted net profits actually improved from the first quarter of 2024 to the first quarter of 2025, rising from $112.8 million to $119.5 million. While operating cash flow plummeted from $125.1 million to $31.9 million, the adjusted figure for it actually grew from $153.9 million to $197.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company did dip, but only slightly, declining from $278.3 million to $276.3 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management anticipates revenue of between $5.10 billion and $5.50 billion. The midpoint there would still translate to a year-over-year drop of 1.7%. However, EBITDA is expected to come in at between $1.08 billion and $1.18 billion. Hitting the midpoint there would mean a year-over-year improvement of 1.3%. And with adjusted earnings per share of between $2.90 and $3.30, we should be looking at adjusted net profits, at the midpoint, of about $455.1 million. Assuming other profitability metrics rise at the same rate, we should anticipate adjusted operating cash flow for the year of about $609.4 million.

Using these estimates, and using the historical results that the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are, to me, quite attractive multiples. They are also attractive compared to other similar companies in the packaging industry. In the table below, you can see Sealed Air Corporation stacked up against five similar firms. And without exception, using any of the three valuation approaches, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Sealed Air Corporation 10.4 7.7 7.9
Sonoco Products Company (SON) 29.3 9.8 46.0
Graphic Packaging Holding Company (GPK) 10.9 10.2 11.6
Ranpak Holdings Corp. (PACK) 137.1 8.7 222.3
Avery Dennison Corporation (AVY) 20.7 18.0 16.3
International Paper Company (IP) 40.5 17.8 87.8

Despite the issues that the firm has experienced, management is working on other ways to improve operations. For instance, this year, it is allocating around 4% of its revenue, or approximately $220 million, toward capital expenditures. 40% of this figure will be dedicated to growth prospects. On top of this, the company is also planning to put about $250 million toward reducing debt. And it is doing that while simultaneously paying out around $120 million of dividends. These are all positive developments, though I would personally prefer that the dividends be allocated toward additional debt reduction or growth prospects instead. But not everything can be perfect.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I do believe that Sealed Air Corporation makes for an attractive prospect. The last couple of years now have certainly been disappointing. This would be doubly true for anybody who owned the stock. Having said that, the company is cheap and improvements are being achieved on the bottom line thanks to restructuring initiatives. While I have no doubt that the current trade war situation could negatively affect the business in the near term, I do believe that, once the dust settles, it should do just fine. As such, I have decided to reaffirm it as a ‘buy’ candidate.

data.title: From Setback To Strength: Forward Air's Bumpy Road To Recovery
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Alps Capital as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Medium-Term Growth Opportunities Through Changing Conditions

Forward Air Corporation (NASDAQ:FWRD) appears undervalued and presents a promising rebound opportunity despite an adjusted loss of $1.12 per share in the first quarter of 2025. Signs of operational improvement, a solid liquidity position of $393 million, and the transformative January 2024 acquisition of Omni Logistics strengthen its long-term outlook. Opportunities in the medium-term are supported by its pricing power amid fluctuating markets. Strategic pricing and initial synergy gains suggest potential for margin recovery. While integration and market volatility present both risks and opportunities, the risk/reward ratio is favorable, as FWRD’s positioning suggests significant upside for medium-term investors.

Company Overview

A brief overview of Forward Air Corporation’s business is a leading logistics provider in North America, operating through its Expedited Freight, Omni Logistics and Intermodal divisions. Its services range from LTL and truckload brokerage to international freight forwarding, enhanced by the acquisition of Omni Logistics, which provides access to a new global customer base. This integration marks a strategic turning point, positioning Forward Air as a global logistics player offering a more comprehensive and relevant range of services, which promotes pricing power.

Financial Performance Highlights

Growing revenue and Strengthening Margins

Forward Air showed the first signs of operational recovery in first quarter 2025, with revenue up 13.2% year-over-year to $613.3 million despite a net loss of $50.6 million as the report highlights. This loss is caused by high integration costs associated with the Omni Logistics acquisition in January 2024, particularly related to systems integration and workforce alignment, which increased general and administrative expenses, something that was anticipated when integrating Omni. Then there’s also the adjusted debt of $1.77 billion of Omni-related interest expense, which further weigh on net income, resulting in a high debt to EBITAD ratio of 5.37x, per the Q1 2025 10-Q. In addition, there's a 10.9% decline in tonnage in the freight segment due to the unpredictable economic environment.

This clearly shows that there has been an integration of the Omni Logistics acquisition to generate synergy within the group, despite some growing pains. Indeed, the revenue of $323.5 million speaks for itself. Compared to other business segments, this one is the best performing in terms of revenue.

Omni Acquisition: Opportunities and Challenges

The January 2024 Omni acquisition added $1.3 billion in total twelve months revenue but increased debt by $1.77 billion and incurred integration costs, putting downward pressure on net income. Moreover, the integration costs for systems and workforce alignment delay profitability.

Forward Air's debt management strategy is to conserve or improve available free cash flow (the $16.4 million Q1 2025 covered 40% of quarterly debt service) and improve the cash balance of $393 million, which cover the difference. Another necessary element of the strategy is to maintaining the Omni Synergies operation and the cost-savings policy, which will improve liquidity. Another option is the sale of one of Forward Air’s segments. While its debt is significant, FWRD’s cash flow generation, and long maturities keep it manageable.

Noticeably, Forward Air’s Freight segment saw its EBITDA margin improve by 400 basis points. This suggests that it is increasing its pricing as expected to compensate for a decline in shipment volumes. While these operational changes illustrate an advantage, the stock market’s time is not the same as the company’s time.

The Intermodal Division and Segment Performance

Let’s now introduce Forward Air’s Intermodal division. It combines road and rail transportation for cost-effective long-distance freight, leveraging partnerships with major railroads to maximize efficiency. It posted solid results this quarter, with revenue up 11% and operating income up 54.5% year-over-year, demonstrating a significant improvement in profitability. Operating margin was 8.9%, reflecting increased efficiency, driven by a 7.4% increase in freight revenue per shipment, which helped increase operating income by 54.5% despite revenue growth of only 11% highlights the importance of the intermodal division in the overall improvement of the in FWRD group.

This highlights a solid effort to achieve operational efficiency gains. Ultimately, Forward Air appears to be moving from simply integrating Omni Logistics to realizing tangible operational benefits, including improved cash flow and profitability. This improvement will take time, which creates volatility as the timing of earnings in this business segment is disrupted, and therefore forecasts must include a loss of visibility into future results. Despite continued market volatility, the company’s recovery is evident, with free cash flow increasing from -$55.8 million to +$16.4 million, supported by strong operating cash generation.

Liquidity and Financial Flexibility

What I find particularly reassuring is Forward Air’s solid liquidity position. With $393 million in available liquidity at the end of Q1 2025, demonstrated that it can generate cash even while navigating integration costs and interest obligations. While the long-term debt of $1.68 billion is significant, i believe it remains under control, especially in light of the company’s ability to generate operating cash flow and maintain covenant compliance. For now, Forward Air appears well equipped to balance its strategic ambitions with prudent financial management.

Risks

Macro Backdrop

As much as Forward Air shows promising signs of recovery, it’s important to stay grounded in the risks ahead. The broader macroeconomic environment, whether marked by inflation or stagflation, is driven by global deficits and shifting trade dynamics. We’re already seeing signs of pressure, like the decline in tonnage within the Expedited Freight segment.

Moreover, regulatory risks add another layer of uncertainty to this already complex environment. Shifts in labor laws, environmental standards or trade policies might have a real consequence, like raising costs or a slowdown freight activity. New tariffs could ripple across global supply chains, and that would hit Omni Logistics right where it matters, given how much it relies on global freight (43.9% of its revenue growth in Q1). With Forward Air already carrying a significant amount of debt, these risks make it clear that the integration of Omni and the company’s ability to stay focused on execution aren’t just strategic goals, they are critical to turning today’s recovery as a foundation for creating lasting strength.

Governance Tensions Reflect Shareholder Frustration

There’s growing tension brewing inside Forward Air. Activist investor Ancora Holdings, which is a large shareholder in the company, has stepped up its efforts to shake up the board, as outlined in its letter to the board written by James Chadwick and Frederick D.DiSanto. With the annual shareholder meeting recently having taken place on June 11, Ancora is using a company rule to its advantage: if directors fail to secure more than 50.1% of shareholder votes, they’re expected to step down. Despite having secured the necessary votes, three directors stepped down anyway. To me, this goes beyond a disagreement over one deal. It shows a deeper frustration with how the board has managed strategic decisions and interacted with shareholders. The next few weeks could be crucial, we might see a significant change in Forward Air’s leadership.

Competitive Scene

Forward Air operates in a highly competitive transportation and logistics industry, facing similar challenges and pressures across its various segments.

In the expedited freight, LTL segment, competitors such as Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), Saia (SAIA) and XPO, Inc. (XPO) pose a formidable challenge to its market share. They operate larger terminal networks and implement advanced technology. These competitors operate significantly more terminals as well and serve a broader customer base across diverse industries. This implies aggressive pricing and threatens Forward Air's margin EBITDA, particularly in the context of a 10.9% decline in tonnage.

In the Omni Logistics segment, competition from global players such as Deutsche Post AG (OTCPK:DHLGY) and DB Schenker (now acquired by DSV), which offer more extensive international networks, pose a significant challenge, putting into question whether Forward Air’s revenue growth of 43.9%, is solid enough. Integration challenges could cause it to lose market share if synergies falter.

Though there is intense competition, Forward Air differentiates itself through its post-Omni vertical integration in different parts of the business. And, it can gain efficiency through automation and IT streamlining.

In the Intermodal division, competitors include JB Hunt Transportation Services (JBHT), Hub Group (HUBG) and railroads such as BNSF (owned by Berkshire Hathaway). The competition is also experiencing revenue growth though at aa much higher level, reflecting a broader customer base and higher shipping volumes, supported by expanded container fleets and rail access. The difference in network scope is a mixed bag. Forward Air’s terminal network likely consists of around 90 facilities, fewer than J.B. Hunt’s 100+ access centers, but more than HUB Group’s 33 terminals or BNSF’s 30 hubs. This limits FWRD’s geographic reach and customer acquisition compared to J.B. Hunt, but positions it well with respect to the others. Additionally, railroads like BNSF, Union Pacific and CSX control the rail infrastructure, making Forward Air's dependent on partnerships, which reduces its bargaining power relative to its integrated competitors.

Indeed, Forward Air's long-term survival in an economic sector where competing giants are difficult to avoid is not an insurmountable feat, especially now in times of uncertainty. Forward Air’s operating margin is competitive, which underlines its cost discipline.

Price Signals a Hold

In my valuation, I will value each of FWRD’s business segments separately: Expedited Freight, Omni Logistics and Intermodal, based on their LTM EBITDA. Then, I will apply EBITDA multiples appropriate to industry peers. Finally, the total enterprise value is adjusted for net debt and cash to determine the equity value, which is divided by the number of shares outstanding to obtain the target price per share.
